quick_facts.label: National occupational qualification
quick_facts.value: Kitchen Trainee is specified by the Tourism & Hospitality Skill Council (THSC) as qualification pack THC/Q2702, version 4.0, at NSQF Level 3 with 13 credits.

quick_facts.label: Core role
quick_facts.value: The role supports Commis Chefs by receiving and storing kitchen supplies, carrying out instructed basic food-preparation work, and maintaining kitchen cleanliness.

quick_facts.label: Sector and work setting
quick_facts.value: THSC classifies the occupation under Tourism & Hospitality, Hotels/Restaurant sub-sector, and Food Production and Kitchen occupation.

quick_facts.label: Training reference point
quick_facts.value: THSC’s version 4.0 model curriculum specifies a 390-hour programme including employability skills and on-the-job training; its curriculum table assigns 30 mandatory OJT hours.

quick_facts.label: Published qualification entry profile
quick_facts.value: The qualification pack lists Grade 10/equivalent as the standard educational route, with specified experience-based alternatives for Grade 8, Grade 9, or a relevant NSQF Level 2 qualification; it lists a minimum job-entry age of 17 years.

sections.heading: What a Kitchen Trainee does
paragraphs: A Kitchen Trainee works under a Commis Chef rather than independently running a kitchen section. The nationally published THSC qualification describes the job as helping with receipt and storage of supplies, basic preparation as instructed, and cleanliness in the kitchen.

The work can include setting up and restocking workstations; collecting ingredients from dry stores, refrigerators, or freezers; washing, peeling, chopping, measuring and mixing ingredients; preparing simple items such as dough or ground spices; and assisting with commercial equipment including stoves, ovens, cookers and fryers. Cleaning and closing tasks include disinfecting work surfaces and tools, cleaning appliances, monitoring instructed holding temperatures, reporting faulty equipment, and handling waste in line with workplace policy.
bullets: Assist with inventory receipt, storage, stock rotation and distribution to kitchen workstations.
Carry out mise en place and basic preparation only to the supervising Commis Chef’s instructions.
Keep equipment, work areas and utensils clean, dry, safe and ready for the next shift.
Escalate damaged equipment, spoiled supplies, or other issues to the supervising chef.

sections.heading: Entry routes in the Indian education and skills system
paragraphs: The most directly aligned skills route is THSC’s Kitchen Trainee qualification, THC/Q2702 version 4.0. Its published eligibility profile uses Grade 10/equivalent as the usual entry point, while allowing defined experience-based alternatives for learners with Grade 8, Grade 9 or a relevant Level 2 qualification. The model curriculum is 390 hours and includes mandatory on-the-job training, making it a structured route for foundational kitchen work.

The linked Diploma in Food Production and Diploma in Bakery and Confectionery pathways can provide broader formal culinary preparation before entry. The Ministry of Tourism identifies IHMs and Food Craft Institutes as providers of technical hospitality training, and its 2025–26 annual report records an ITDC/NIOS Diploma in Food Production and Bakery programme. Specific admission criteria, duration, fees, recognition and seat availability must be checked with the delivering institute for the applicable intake.
bullets: Skills route: complete the THSC Kitchen Trainee curriculum and assessment for THC/Q2702.
Diploma route: pursue food-production or bakery/confectionery training through an appropriate hospitality-training provider, then seek supervised entry-level kitchen roles.
Work-based route: employers may provide induction and practical training; the THSC curriculum itself includes a mandatory on-the-job component.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: The technical foundation is safe, accurate preparation under supervision: ingredient identification and quality checks, correct storage and temperature procedures, stock rotation, food-preparation techniques such as chopping and slicing, and basic cooking methods. The qualification also expects trainees to organise a hygienic workstation, use kitchen equipment safely and communicate effectively with colleagues and supervisors.

Food safety is not an optional add-on. THSC explicitly includes health, hygiene and safety in the qualification, while FSSAI guidance for food handlers covers personal hygiene, receiving, storage, safe food handling, cleaning and sanitation, waste disposal and pest control. FSSAI’s FoSTaC materials also describe food-handler training as focused on hygienic and sanitary practices under Schedule 4 of the food-business licensing and registration regulations.
bullets: Mise en place, measuring, washing, peeling, chopping, mixing and simple dough or spice preparation.
Storage discipline: labelling, stock rotation, quality checks and temperature awareness.
Hygiene and sanitation: personal cleanliness, disinfection of work areas and safe waste handling.
Safe use and basic checking of tools, knives, refrigeration and cooking equipment.
Clear workplace communication, attention to detail, patience and stamina for shift-based kitchen work.

sections.heading: Where the role is performed
paragraphs: THSC places Kitchen Trainee in the Hotels/Restaurant sub-sector and Food Production and Kitchen occupation. In practice, the work is organised around operational kitchen areas: stores, refrigerators and freezers, preparation benches, cooking stations, dish and utensil areas, and cleaning or waste-handling points. Work is supervised by a Commis Chef and follows the employer’s standard operating procedures.

The qualification’s personal-attribute profile highlights stamina for long hours, patience and attention to detail. Candidates should therefore expect standing work, timed preparation and cleaning routines, heat and equipment hazards, and shift handovers rather than a desk-based role.
bullets: Hotels and restaurants are the formal sub-sector classification for this role.
The role involves close coordination with Commis Chefs and the wider kitchen brigade.
Food-safety, equipment-safety and cleanliness procedures are integral to everyday work.

sections.heading: Likely progression after trainee level
paragraphs: A Kitchen Trainee can build toward Commis work as capability and employer experience grow. THSC places Kitchen Trainee at NSQF Level 3 and Commis at Level 4; the Commis qualification expands responsibility to setting up kitchen operations, preparing food and base items, monitoring stock, and closing the kitchen at shift end.

Further THSC role specifications show a higher-responsibility sequence of Demi Chef de Partie at Level 4.5 and Chef de Partie at Level 5. A Demi Chef de Partie prepares ingredients in an allotted section, guides Commis staff and assists with presentation, stock and quality; a Chef de Partie takes responsibility for food-production and inventory operations in a designated section and assists with people and administrative management. This is a qualification-based career map, not a guarantee of promotion: actual advancement depends on demonstrated competence, vacancies, cuisine or section specialisation, and employer standards.
bullets: Kitchen Trainee — NSQF Level 3: supervised foundational kitchen operations.
Commis — NSQF Level 4: broader preparation and kitchen-operation responsibility.
Demi Chef de Partie — NSQF Level 4.5: section-level preparation, quality and guidance of Commis staff.
Chef de Partie — NSQF Level 5: designated-section production, inventory and supervisory responsibility.
